name: Cao Cao (Mengde)
page_title: Cao Cao (Mengde) 曹操 (孟德) [Han, Wei] – Romance of the Three Kingdoms Encyclopedia – Kongming’s Archives
ranks: Colonel in Charge of the Army
Intendant of the Affairs of the Masters of Writing
novel_notes:
Differences Between Fact and Common Fiction
	
		
	Never presented Dong Zhuo the “Seven-Star Sword.”
	Wen Chou was killed in one of Cao Cao’s military plots.
	There is no historic mention of Cao Cao killing civilians as he marched on Tao Qian, however the massacre he left in departure is recorded.
	Cao Cao did not execute He Yi. [ZZTJ: 62]
	Became Prime Minister on the 6th month of Jian’an (AD 208). Not earlier as presented in the novel.
	Was not tricked by Pang Tong into tying his ships at Chibi. He did this himself because his soldiers were sick.
	Did not execute Zuo Ci, nor did Zuo Ci predict his death.
	Had 25 sons; far from the five mentioned in the novel.

sections.novel:
Differences Between Fact and Common Fiction
	
		
	Never presented Dong Zhuo the “Seven-Star Sword.”
	Wen Chou was killed in one of Cao Cao’s military plots.
	There is no historic mention of Cao Cao killing civilians as he marched on Tao Qian, however the massacre he left in departure is recorded.
	Cao Cao did not execute He Yi. [ZZTJ: 62]
	Became Prime Minister on the 6th month of Jian’an (AD 208). Not earlier as presented in the novel.
	Was not tricked by Pang Tong into tying his ships at Chibi. He did this himself because his soldiers were sick.
	Did not execute Zuo Ci, nor did Zuo Ci predict his death.
	Had 25 sons; far from the five mentioned in the novel.

sections.short-biography: Founder of Wei. Built the foundation of what would become the Jin dynasty.
sections.details:
Traditional Chinese 
	曹操 (孟德)


	 Simplified Chinese 
	曹操 (孟德)


	 Pronunciation 
	Cao2 Cao1 (Meng4de2) 


	 Pinyin (Tones) 
	Cáo Cāo (Mèngdé)


	 Wade-Giles 
	Ts‘ao Ts‘ao (Mêng-tê)

	 Birthplace 
	Qiao County, Pei Commandery (Presently Bozhou city, Anhui Province)

	 Other Names 
	Grand Progenitor (太祖) [posthumous title], Martial Emperor (武帝) [posthumous title], A-man (阿瞞) [child name], Cao Man (曹瞞) [insult], General Cao (曹將), Excellency Cao (曹公) [title]

	 Name Notes 
	In Sanguozhi Chen Shou references him with the honorary title, Wu Di (武帝), or ‘Martial Emperor’. Cao Cao appears in other sources under this name. Cao Cao’s infant name was A-Man (阿瞞).

sections.popular-quotations:
Cao Cao said, “Better that I betray the world than let the world betray me.”[操曰：宁教我负天下人，休教天下人负我。]
			Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 4
		
	
				
		
			Fighting Ma Chao, Cao Cao said, “You despised me because I had no wall to my camp, but lo! in one single night, God has made me a wall. Do you not think it time to give in?”[操曰：“汝欺我營寨不成，今一夜天使築就，汝何不早降！”]
			Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 59
		
	
				
		
			Cao Cao said, “I like to kill people in my dreams, so when I fall asleep be sure not to come too close.”
			Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 72

sections.biography-source-undefined: Cao Cao, styled Mengde, was from Qiao county, Peiguo commandery. He was a son of Cao Song. Cao Song was adopted by the eunuch Cao Teng, something the enemies of Cao Cao were keen on pointing out.

full_text:
Cao Cao (Mengde) 曹操 (孟德)
ARomance of the Three KingdomsEncyclopedia officer profile.
Cao Cao (曹操), styledMengde (孟德), was born in Qiao County, Pei Commandery (Presently Bozhou city, Anhui Province). Founder of Wei. Built the foundation of what would become the Jin dynasty. Cao Cao was affiliated with the Han Dynasty and the Wei Kingdom. Cao Cao lived during the Later Han Dynasty and Three Kingdoms periods of Chinese history.
Return to theThree Kingdoms Encyclopediaor browse theEncyclopedia Directory.
Return toThree Kingdoms Encyclopedia

Founder of Wei. Built the foundation of what would become the Jin dynasty.

Fact vs Fiction
Differences Between Fact and Common Fiction
Never presented Dong Zhuo the “Seven-Star Sword.”
Wen Chou was killed in one of Cao Cao’s military plots.
There is no historic mention of Cao Cao killing civilians as he marched on Tao Qian, however the massacre he left in departure is recorded.
Cao Cao did not execute He Yi. [ZZTJ: 62]
Became Prime Minister on the 6th month of Jian’an (AD 208). Not earlier as presented in the novel.
Was not tricked by Pang Tong into tying his ships at Chibi. He did this himself because his soldiers were sick.
Did not execute Zuo Ci, nor did Zuo Ci predict his death.
Had 25 sons; far from the five mentioned in the novel.
Literary Appearances
Romance of the Three Kingdoms:1–6,10–77,78,79–82,85,88,91,93–94,96–97,99,106,109,111,114,119–120
Sanguozhi: Shu 1-8,Wei 1, Wei 2-30
Popular Quotations
Cao Cao said,“Better that I betray the world than let the world betray me.”
[操曰：宁教我负天下人，休教天下人负我。]
Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 4
Fighting Ma Chao, Cao Cao said,“You despised me because I had no wall to my camp, but lo! in one single night, God has made me a wall. Do you not think it time to give in?”
[操曰：“汝欺我營寨不成，今一夜天使築就，汝何不早降！”]
Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 59
Cao Cao said,“I like to kill people in my dreams, so when I fall asleep be sure not to come too close.”
Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 72

BiographySource Undefined
Cao Cao, styled Mengde, was from Qiao county, Peiguo commandery. He was a son of Cao Song. Cao Song was adopted by the eunuch Cao Teng, something the enemies of Cao Cao were keen on pointing out.
